typescript record meaning example

1 hour ago

In TypeScript, the 

`Record<Keys, Type>` is a **utility type** used to define an object's structure by specifying exactly what its keys and values should be. ![LogRocket Blog](data:image/png;base64,)LogRocket Blog +1

It is essentially a shorthand for creating a "dictionary" or "map" where all values share the same type. ![geshan.com.np](data:image/png;base64,)geshan.com.np +1

Basic Syntax

typescript

```
Record<K, T>
```

Use code with caution.

-   **`K` (Keys):** The type of the property keys (must be `string`, `number`, or `symbol`).
-   **`T` (Type):** The type of the values associated with those keys. ![Convex](data:image/png;base64,)Convex +3

Common Examples

1\. Simple Dictionary

If you want an object where any string can be a key and every value must be a number:

typescript

```
const scores: Record<string, number> = {
  "Alice": 10,
  "Bob": 15
};
```

Use code with caution.

2\. Restricting Keys (Union Types)

You can use `Record` to ensure an object contains a specific set of keys. This is one of its most powerful uses. ![Stack Overflow](data:image/png;base64,)Stack Overflow +2

typescript

```
type Page = "home" | "about" | "contact";

interface PageInfo {
  title: string;
}

// Every key in 'Page' MUST be present in this object
const nav: Record<Page, PageInfo> = {
  home: { title: "Welcome Home" },
  about: { title: "About Us" },
  contact: { title: "Get in Touch" }
};
```

Use code with caution.

3\. Using Enums as Keys

`Record` is often used with enums to ensure every possible case is handled. ![Mimo](data:image/png;base64,)Mimo +1

typescript

```
enum TaskStatus {
  Todo = "TODO",
  InProgress = "IN_PROGRESS",
  Done = "DONE"
}

const statusColors: Record<TaskStatus, string> = {
  [TaskStatus.Todo]: "red",
  [TaskStatus.InProgress]: "orange",
  [TaskStatus.Done]: "green"
};
```

Use code with caution.

Why use `Record` over a standard interface?

-   **Brevity:** It is much faster to write `Record<string, number>` than defining a full index signature like `{ [key: string]: number }`.
-   **Exhaustiveness:** When used with a union (like in the "Page" example above), TypeScript will throw an error if you **forget** to include one of the keys or add an extra one.
-   **Mapping:** It is ideal for "transforming" one type into another (e.g., taking a list of IDs and mapping them to User objects). ![Stack Overflow](data:image/png;base64,)Stack Overflow +4

Would you like to see how to combine **Record** with other utility types like **Partial** or **Readonly**?
